fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out this filesystem abstraction library we've been talking about. The basic idea is that a caller passes in a URL and the library figures out where to get the data — could be local disk, could be over the wire, could be in a bucket, could be a repo, could be in memory. All of these should feel identical from the outside. We've done something similar before with that URL parsing approach we used in the datasource routing work, so just follow that same pattern.

The tricky parts are around the repo URLs — there's a special double-slash convention for splitting the repo location from the file path inside it, and we need to handle the version pinning via the URL hash. Auth for repos is also a bit fiddly: environment variables can inject credentials, and there's that base64 key thing for SSH. For cloud buckets we need to strip out query params that the storage drivers don't understand and keep only the ones they care about. For config values there's a file indirection pattern (the _FILE suffix thing) that mirrors what we did in the secrets loader.

The whole thing needs to be split into separate files per backend — no monoliths please. The stdin/stdout JSON dispatch is just a thin wrapper, the core library shouldn't know about it. Let's make sure unknown schemes and bad URLs give back clean typed errors rather than panics.

trigger_keywords: how do you split repo path from subpath
double slash delimiter meaning
how to separate repository location from file path inside it
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The path is split on the first occurrence of `//` (two consecutive forward slashes). Everything before `//` is the repository location; everything after it becomes the in-repository subpath, re-prefixed with a single `/`. A trailing slash on the subpath is stripped unless the result would be empty, in which case the subpath is `/`. When there is no `//` in the path, the entire path is the repository and the subpath defaults to `/`. When the path starts with `//`, the repository portion is the empty string and the remainder (with a leading `/`) is the subpath.

trigger_keywords: how to expand a bare branch name to a full ref
what happens when the URL fragment has no refs/ prefix
how is the git ref derived from the URL hash
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The version reference is taken from the URL fragment (the part after `#`). If the fragment already begins with `refs/` it is used verbatim. If the fragment is a bare name (does not start with `refs/`) it is expanded to `refs/heads/<name>`. If the URL has no fragment at all the resulting reference is the empty string, reported as `ref=`.

trigger_keywords: what schemes are treated as git backends
how is a git+ssh or git+http URL handled
how does the router strip the git+ prefix
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A URL whose scheme carries a `git+` prefix (e.g. `git+ssh`, `git+http`, `git+https`) is routed to the version-control backend and the `git+` prefix is stripped to derive the underlying transport scheme, which is reported as `repo_scheme`. Plain `git` and `ssh` schemes are also routed to the version-control backend. The in-repository root is extracted from the path using the `//` split rule; only the subpath portion becomes `root`.

trigger_keywords: what auth method is chosen when only a username is in the URL
difference between token auth and basic auth from environment
when does GIT_HTTP_TOKEN win over a password
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the URL carries both a username and a password in the userinfo section, basic authentication is selected with those credentials. When the URL carries only a username (no password) over `http`/`https`, the environment is consulted: if `GIT_HTTP_TOKEN` is set, token authentication is selected and only the token (`secret`) is reported — the username is not included. If instead a password-like variable is set, basic authentication is selected. For `ssh` scheme URLs, if `GIT_SSH_KEY` is set (raw PEM or base64-encoded), SSH key authentication is selected and the connecting user from the URL is reported. `file` scheme URLs and `http`/`https` URLs with no userinfo at all resolve to `kind=none`.

trigger_keywords: which query parameters are kept for s3 URLs
what params does the gs backend recognise
how are unknown query parameters removed from bucket URLs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For `s3` URLs the only retained query parameters are `region`, `endpoint`, `disableSSL`, and `s3ForcePathStyle`. For `gs` URLs the only retained parameters are `access_id` and `private_key_path`. All other parameters are discarded entirely. The surviving parameters are re-encoded in canonical (alphabetically sorted) order. If no recognised parameters survive the query string is omitted from the output URL entirely.

trigger_keywords: how does the _FILE env var indirection work
what happens when the file named by KEY_FILE is missing
precedence order for getenv resolution
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Resolution precedence is: (1) if the exact key is present in the environment, return its value immediately; (2) otherwise, if `<KEY>_FILE` is set in the environment, open and read that file and return its contents trimmed of leading/trailing whitespace; (3) if `<KEY>_FILE` points to a file that does not exist or cannot be read, fall through to the default; (4) return the caller-supplied default, or the empty string if no default was given.

trigger_keywords: what object-storage schemes are supported
how is an s3 or gs URL routed
what is the blob backend root field
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The object-storage (blob) backend handles the schemes `s3`, `gs`, and `azblob`. The router reports `backend=blob`, `scheme=<original scheme>`, and `root=<bucket-relative path>`. The bucket-relative root is the URL path with the leading slash and the bucket-name segment removed — for `s3://foo/bar` the bucket is `foo` and the root is `bar`.

trigger_keywords: how should invalid or unparseable URLs be reported
what error is returned for an unknown scheme
how are routing errors communicated to callers
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Two distinct error conditions exist. A URL that cannot be parsed at all (malformed syntax) yields `error=invalid_url` with no additional fields. A URL that is syntactically valid but whose scheme is not supported yields `error=unsupported_scheme` together with `scheme=<the offending scheme>`. These are reported as typed, structured errors — not panics or generic strings — so callers can branch on the error kind.

trigger_keywords: should the JSON dispatch logic be inside the core library
where does stdin/stdout parsing live
how is the execution adapter separated from the filesystem logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The core library must have no knowledge of stdin, stdout, or JSON. A dedicated, thin execution adapter (a separate file/package) reads JSON commands from stdin, calls into the core library, and writes the key=value text results to stdout. The core library exposes idiomatic file operations (read, stat, list, glob, sub-tree) returning typed values and typed errors. This separation is mandatory — mixing I/O formatting into the core violates the stated SRP constraint.

trigger_keywords: what filesystem operations must every backend support
what is the minimum interface a backend must implement
does every backend need to support glob and list
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The uniform read-only filesystem interface includes: `Open`/`Read` (retrieve full file bytes by relative path), `Stat` (name, size, is_dir, and where applicable content-type and mod-time), `ReadDir`/`List` (enumerate entries at a path, each with name and file-or-directory type), and `Glob` (return sorted matching paths for a shell pattern). Not every backend is required to expose every operation if the underlying medium makes it impossible, but each backend must be substitutable wherever the interface is expected without callers needing to know the concrete type.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what values can op take
list all supported op values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'git_read', 'git_stat', 'git_list', 'billy_read', 'billy_list', 'route', 'split_path', 'resolve_ref', 'select_auth', 'normalize_url', 'getenv', 'file_read', 'file_stat', 'file_list', 'file_glob', 'http_read', 'http_stat', 'http_content_type', 'http_missing', 'blob_read', 'blob_stat', 'blob_list'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that URL parsing approach we used in the datasource routing work
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The scheme-based router in `router.go` (or equivalent) that inspects `url.URL.Scheme`, strips `git+` prefixes, and maps schemes to backend constants — mirroring the pattern described in Feature 1 (feature1_url_routing.json) and Feature 2 (feature2_repo_path_split.json)

context_pointers.vague_reference: the file indirection pattern (the _FILE suffix thing) that mirrors what we did in the secrets loader
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `GetenvWithDefault` / `getenv` helper described in Feature 6 (feature6_env_value_resolution.json): check env key directly, then fall back to reading the file path stored in `<KEY>_FILE`, then fall back to the supplied default

context_pointers.vague_reference: that base64 key thing for SSH
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `GIT_SSH_KEY` environment variable handling in the auth-selection logic (feature4_git_auth_selection.json): the value may be a raw PEM block or a base64-encoded PEM block; the library must decode it to obtain the private key bytes before selecting `kind=ssh_key`

context_pointers.vague_reference: the double-slash convention for splitting the repo location from the file path
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `SplitRepoPath` function (or equivalent) tested in feature2_repo_path_split.json: finds the first `//` in the URL path string, assigns everything before it as `repo` and everything after (prefixed with `/`) as `subpath`

context_pointers.vague_reference: strip out query params that the storage drivers don't understand
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The bucket URL normalizer in feature5_bucket_url_normalization.json: per-scheme allowlists (`s3` keeps `region`, `endpoint`, `disableSSL`, `s3ForcePathStyle`; `gs` keeps `access_id`, `private_key_path`) with all other params dropped and survivors re-encoded in sorted order

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's not something I have specific guidance on right now. Please check the existing backend implementations and the test fixtures under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for the relevant feature — the expected input/output pairs there are the authoritative contract. If you're still unsure, flag it in the PR and we can discuss.

feature10_1_git_read.json.description: Clone a version-control repository on demand and read the committed contents of a file by relative path.

feature10_2_git_stat.json.description: Report metadata (name, byte size, directory flag) for a committed file in a version-control-backed filesystem.

feature10_3_git_list.json.description: List the entries at a path in a version-control-backed filesystem, reporting each name/type and the total count.

feature11_1_inmemory_read.json.description: Read the full byte contents of a file by relative path from a filesystem adapted from an in-memory tree, including deeply nested files.

feature11_2_inmemory_list.json.description: List the directory entries of a filesystem adapted from an in-memory tree, reporting each name/type and the total count.

feature1_url_routing.json.description: Resolve a source URL string to a backend category and the routing-relevant fields the router derives from it; reject malformed URLs and unknown schemes.

feature2_repo_path_split.json.description: Split a source path into the repository portion and the in-repository subpath, delimited by a doubled slash; when no delimiter is present the whole path is the repository and the subpath is root.

feature3_git_ref_resolution.json.description: Derive a fully-qualified version-control reference name from a source URL fragment: a bare name becomes a branch reference, a fragment already starting with the reference prefix is preserved verbatim, and no fragment yields an empty reference.

feature4_git_auth_selection.json.description: Choose an authentication method for a version-control source. URL userinfo provides basic credentials; environment variables can supply a password, a bearer token, or a private key; schemes without credentials select no authentication. The chosen method is reported as a neutral kind plus any user/secret fields.

feature5_bucket_url_normalization.json.description: Sanitize an object-storage URL by retaining only the query parameters meaningful to the storage backend and discarding all others; the remaining parameters are re-encoded in canonical order.

feature6_env_value_resolution.json.description: Resolve a configuration value by key: prefer the directly-set value, otherwise read and trim the contents of the file named by the '<KEY>_FILE' indirection, otherwise fall back to the supplied default (or empty).

feature7_1_local_read.json.description: Read the full byte contents of a file from the local-directory-backed filesystem by relative path.

feature7_2_local_stat.json.description: Report metadata (name, byte size, directory flag) for a file in the local-directory-backed filesystem.

feature7_3_local_list.json.description: List the directory entries at a path in the local-directory-backed filesystem, reporting each entry's name and whether it is a file or directory, plus the total count.

feature7_4_local_glob.json.description: Return the sorted set of paths in the local-directory-backed filesystem that match a shell-style glob pattern, plus the match count.

feature8_1_http_read.json.description: Fetch the body of a resource from the HTTP-backed filesystem by relative path using a GET request.

feature8_2_http_stat.json.description: Report metadata for an HTTP resource obtained via a HEAD request: name, content length, content type, last-modified time, and directory flag.

feature8_3_http_content_type.json.description: Negotiate content type with the HTTP backend by sending an Accept header; the server echoes it back and the reported content type reflects the negotiated value.

feature8_4_http_missing.json.description: Accessing a non-existent HTTP resource fails with a normalized error carrying the protocol status code rather than content.

feature9_1_blob_read.json.description: Read the full contents of an object from a bucket-backed filesystem by key, including objects nested under a key prefix.

feature9_2_blob_stat.json.description: Report metadata (name, size, directory flag, octal permission bits) for an object in a bucket-backed filesystem.

feature9_3_blob_list.json.description: List the entries under a prefix in a bucket-backed filesystem, distinguishing objects (files) from synthetic sub-prefixes (directories), reporting each name/type and the total count. Listing can also be rooted at a key prefix.

name=dir1 type=dir
name=dir2 type=dir
name=file1 type=file
name=file2 type=file
name=file3 type=file

name=file3 type=file
name=file4 type=file
name=sub1 type=dir
